title: YouTube removed 7m channels, Zeck Felms banned | YT NEWS #1

transcript.text: The most important news of the week is that one of our portfolio channels got falsely terminated. I'm talking about Zack Felms. I was in the middle of the call when my partner called me saying that the channel was completely removed. And even after we crafted an appeal connected with our MCN, we still got an email from an AI review that the channel is not going to be restored. That's honestly when I had had enough of it and I tweeted this whole situation. Fortunately, I have a great audience on Twitter. I was able to ask Zack Film's fans uh to retweet it and uh share our story so that YouTube notices it. Um and uh this is where I have to give credit to them because everyone was just spamming the hashtag freezacelms. And after a day of waiting, YouTube finally reinstated the channel saying, "Our team has reviewed this and we are happy to let you know that your mentioned channel has been fully reinstated as it does not violate our community guidelines. Thanks for your patience while we looked into this." And even though the whole team was super happy to to hear all of that, the situation is still very crazy because uh AI is completely out of control. We saw so many terminations this and last week and uh channels are getting terminated for some reasons like in our case it was impersonating like we create original 3D content and still somehow the AI thinks that we impersonated uh someone. So AI is out of control. It makes decisions. It then confirms it uh through the appeal process. And uh it's impossible to get your channel back if you don't have an audience. If you don't have uh the uh supporters who would uh then go on Twitter on a third-party tool uh to just let YouTube know that they for whatever reason falsely terminated the channel. So you better build an audience on Twitter if you want to be um reinstated on YouTube once your channel gets banned. >> [music] [music] [music] >> Welcome to the second YouTube news digest, where I'm going to give you the niches, the channel updates, uh the YouTube news, and basically everything you need uh in order for you to figure out Faceless YouTube and just stay on top of the game uh even if you are already a player of it. So without further ado, let's get started. The video that we posted on the falsely terminated and then reinstated channel blew up to 3.3 million views. So uh this is the biggest problem of channels getting terminated uh is that even if they get reinstated the views drop almost to zero because YouTube silently shadowbans all of the reinstated profiles just to protect uh its advertisers from uh spam and scam and all of those things. But seems like we got lucky because uh when we posted this video without expecting anything uh on the next day it blew up to 2 million and now it sits at around 3.3 uh million views and overall the channel real time got back to normal and we're back to making $500 days on this channel. The Art of War channel is slowly climbing back after the shadow ban. As you remember, I'm reviving one of my uh main channels from an age restriction that I got months ago. And uh uh this week, we got some massive one of 10 on the channel. Like for example, this video that did 2.9 million views in 14 hours. And the funny thing about this one is that it was stuck at 300 views for half an hour before finally exploding to amazing numbers like this. Max Versstappen lost the F1 championship by only two points. And the only reason why it's important in the YouTube digest is because I spent last weekend attending the last race of the 2025 season rooting for Max. Got a bit disappointed at the end, of course. But when I came back home, I realized that the portfolio of my YouTube channels still made $4.5,000 over the weekend. My goal till the end of this year is to get this portfolio to over 100K a month. Right now, our run rate is around $17,000. Still need a 30% increase, but very happy with the results that we are seeing so far. Matthew Beam reveals how much money he makes from YouTube and uh it's not as crazy as you think. So, first, Matthew talked about uh the costs of some of his videos. The video where he transformed Mr. Beast Wool, for example, cost him $30,000. transforming speeds bus was $18,000 with an average cost of videos about $19,000 last year. But the true surprise to me was uh hearing how much he actually made from AdSense because he says that he generates on average $50,000 a month from AdSense. That to me sounds ridiculous because uh we were able to achieve the same exact numbers with YouTube shorts without having to find ways to talk to celebrities, shoot this Mr. bis style videos have 8 million subs and we did it with a crazy 65% margin which I guess goes to show that uh if you want to be a YouTuber try faceless YouTube first because it might be more profitable while not having the downside of being famous YouTube AI will create an entirely new class of creators that today cannot do it because they don't have the skill or the equipment. So, YouTube SEO in his recent interview with Time revealed a few interesting things about AI. Number one, they're not against it. As I was saying on the last episode, they're actually aware and [snorts] happy that AI exists on their platform. And what he's saying next is there will be good AI content and bad AI content, meaning loweffort, scam, spam kind of videos. And it will be up to YouTube and our investment in technology and the algorithms to bring the good one to the four. In short, if you're creating good highquality AI videos, YouTube is going to push those videos to new audiences. But if you're creating AI slop like this one is going to ban or shadowban you. Richard from one of 10. YouTube titles with less than six words massively outperform everything else. So research analyzed 300,000 viral YouTube videos trying to find out how long your YouTube titles should be. And they found that a title with five words performs almost 50% better than a title with 15 words. Generally speaking, the shorter your title, the more million views it gets based on uh the research. He also suggests the formula where you craft a short punchy title and then add context using the thumbnail. And I actually very much agree with that because we are using the same strategy for hooks in our YouTube shorts. The shorter and punchier it is, the more views the video typically gets. Viewers don't have too much time to think and consider clicking on your video. They need a huge spark of curiosity in order for them to do it. YouTube creators can now test three different thumbnails and titles concurrently. This is one of the massive news uh YouTube dropped this week. Before you can only test three different thumbnails, but because your titles are just as important, YouTube now allows you to test three different variations of each. It reminds me of Meta that allows you to do the same thing with your Facebook ads, but YouTube is actually the first platform to apply the same principle to organic. Worth mentioning that the way YouTube is going to decide that uh the thumbnail and title is better than the other one not by tracking the CTR your click-through rate but by looking at the watch time as the single most important metric that shows the audience satisfaction during a session. YouTube SEO AI will make our ability to detect and enforce on violative content better, more precise, and able to cope with scale. Well, like many SEOs, he says one thing, but actually means another. And if I were to describe how the new AI detection system works, it basically silence anything that is even slightly close to the line because they would rather ban a few innocent channels than leave a small number of spam channels alive. Because at the end of the day, all platforms typically care about uh one specific thing, protecting the revenue streams uh and relationships with their advertisers. So AI will only make your work as a creator harder. And you better watch those news episodes to see what other people are getting banned for so you can avoid all of those things. YouTube removed 7,456,000 channels in the last 6 months. Besides that, AI also removed 74 million YouTube videos, which just gets to show the point I was making about the YouTube SEO, but 7.5 million channels. Let that think poly market. Jake Paul could become one of the best performing venture investors over the last 10 years. He invested in ramp seed round in 2019 now valued at $32 billion. Invested in Chronosphere seed round in 2019 that is now valued at $3.3 billion. Then invested in Underreal $8.4 $4 billion variation that is now valued at 30 billion. Uh and then invested in Ollipop at 200 million uh that is now valued at 1.8 billion. Well, of course, Jake is not choosing the companies to invest in by himself. But what it shows is that the moment you have attention, it's very easy for you to cash it out. like Ryan Reynolds who buys small companies, uses his brand and name to promote them and become their face and then sells them a few years later for four to 10x return. Same strategy worked for uh Jake. And with Faceless YouTube, you can actually do the exact same thing while staying completely off the radar, which is why it's so attractive. Mr. BIS and BIS industries is projected to generate $375 million in profit this year. So this week there were a lot of rumors that BIS industries is going uh to go public. If that happens, they're going to have a valuation of 1.5 uh billion. But uh one of the sources actually shows that table for best industries acquired from the uh New York country clerk. And uh in those reports you can see that best industries is actually very very profitable even with how much Jimmy invests uh in his videos. Um and actually I was a bit surprised because 2025 videos from Mr. Bis were underperforming drastically compared to the videos uh from Mr. Bis that he posted in 2024. But it looks like uh it didn't stop Jimmy from uh generating more than $30 million in profit every single months. Leno's T channel generated around $26 million from YouTube AdSense over its lifetime. And Sebastian also added that ad revenue only makes up about 10% of the company's earnings. By the way, Linux is a perfect blueprint for building a modern company. And here's what you need to know uh to build one. Number one, pick a niche that is complicated enough so you naturally have fever competitors. Number two, reinvest AdSense revenue in a team so they create better videos. Number three, better videos bring in more viewers. Number four, more viewers watch longer and form real trust. Then the trust opens the doors for sponsors, merch, hardware, and new shows. and more viewers bring in even more ad revenue. Then you reinvest the revenue into building even better team uh better gear and more ambitious ideas. And uh the ideas improve the content which then leads to more viewers and the cycle uh repeats itself. So this way YouTube AdSense revenue basically acts like the channel's uh VC giving it just enough cash at the beginning to turn it into a real enterprise. So, the craziest niche leak of the week is the Majestic AI Studio, the channel that generated $3,000 on a single video posted just two days ago. So, this channel is doing AI video stories with ideas that they steal from Agent Floppy, another uh 2D animated faceless channel. Uh, and they were doing decent. I mean, five videos, a million views, around $6 to $7,000 made. Uh, but then they post this video and everything changes. They try a new format and the video explodes to 450,000 views in 36 hours. It generates them about $3,000 and it's incredibly easy to replicate. The original idea is a tour of London in 1700s. You can run a dedicated channel for tours and create videos on New York, Egypt, Napoleonic Aquarius, World War II, and many other moments in history uh that uh you can recreate and visualize with AI. And what's really important is that the comments under this video actually say that this is how AI should be used. Meaning, the audience absolutely loved the format. And again, I haven't seen it anywhere. It's brand new and it's still very underrated. But as usual, AI niches are dying out very quickly. So this niche has uh about two months before it gets fluided with other uh AI competitors. So if you want to run a channel like that, I think the potential here is 40 to $120,000 in the next uh 3 months. But you have to be quick. Another crazy niche leak. This YouTube channel posted four videos, generated 10 million views, and made $60,000 with less than $200 in expenses. Uh so, it's very rare to see a channel blow up uh so fast, but uh here's what they did with their videos. They basically target the same people who would go uh soul cycling. Basically, they create motivational music videos, and the video itself is a combination of AI clips, stock footage, and text on the screen, which is very easy to replicate. Uh, these videos will literally cost you5 to $10. Uh, but some of them uh, can generate up to 9 million long form views. And you know me, I'm not a fan of the short-term niches, but if you need to make your first money with YouTube, this could be the opportunity you were searching for. But, uh, again, you have to be very quick because the niche will get saturated within weeks. Another super easy niche to run this week. This channel was getting shitty views on their motivational AI videos, but then they switched to using cartoons instead. And this new format blew up to 1.3 million views on their first ever video. Since then, the channel exploded, and for the last 3 months, it was consistently generating uh $12,000 while only posting a video every week. These videos are extremely easy to make. You need a VA to source the footage and then an editor to slightly change it or you can even do it yourself in Capcot if that's the first YouTube channel that you run. Talking Hut channel generated about $130,000 showing the most painful death ever. So this is the format that uh they copied from Paint Explainer that's going absolutely crazy for almost anyone who tries it. But what's interesting about them is that they're not actually using uh 2D animation. Instead, they made motion comic that's like two times as cheap as animations. So, this way they were able to bring their expenses down while still having a barrier to entry in the niche. As for the stats, the channel posted 23 videos, got 25 million views, over a million views on average, and generated around $130,000 uh lifetime. With a proper team and system, you can simply outperform them by uh posting more. It will cost you around $2,000 a month to run the channel, but will most likely generate $30 to $40,000 with consistent posting. For anyone who wants to start their first branded YouTube channel, this is an amazing niche to do so. Niche leak. A YouTube channel made $9,000 simply revealing chess tricks. Tony Animation was a struggling creator trying to enter the 3D niche that I'm in, but nothing really worked for him. Now, he privated all of his old videos, but I remember this channel struggling to get more than 20 30,000 views, uh, which with shorts is not a lot. But then, one of his videos about chess exploded and he turned it into his niche. Now, he creates chess breakdown videos in 3D, which is unique, branded, and has no competitors. His best YouTube short got 22 million views, making him about $2,000 from a simple clip. But what's more interesting is that he started doing lawn form three weeks ago and his first ever video is already at 200k views. Good job, Tony. This is where the real money are. And for everyone watching, don't touch Tony. Don't create another chess channel, but instead use his strategy to find a unique angle that was never covered before and create niche for yourself just like Tony did. Oh, and by the way, Tony, I know that you are one of my uh fans because your strategy really reminds me of mine. So, if you're watching this, reach out. I'd like to invest in your channel and help you scale past 42K months uh using our teams and systems. I know you're watching this, so DM me on Axe and uh let's make that happen. You have an amazing channel.
